proof_trace: sass_value_get_tag() itself (sass_values.cpp:17) makes zero allocations — it is a trivial getter. However, in c2ast() (the function calling it), SASS_LIST and SASS_MAP cases allocate via raw SASS_MEMORY_NEW into unguarded raw pointers (List* l, Map* m). If any recursive c2ast() call throws — triggered when a list/map element has tag SASS_ERROR or SASS_WARNING (which calls error() unconditionally) — the containing List* or Map* object is leaked because no try/catch or RAII wrapper guards the allocation. Smart pointer aliases List_Obj/Map_Obj exist but are not used at these sites.
attack_scenario: A custom C function registered via the libsass plugin API returns a SASS_LIST or SASS_MAP value containing a nested SASS_ERROR element; the top-level check in eval.cpp:1103-1112 passes because the outer tag is SASS_LIST, c2ast() allocates the List container, then throws on the nested error element, leaking the container and any already-appended child nodes each call.

proof_trace: `size_t* state = new size_t[L + 1]` is allocated at line 27, but the early return `return {}` at line 32 (triggered when any inner vector is empty) exits before the sole `delete[] state` at line 67. No RAII wrapper or smart pointer is used, so the allocation leaks on this path. The caller in `extender.cpp:614` only guards against an empty outer vector, leaving the inner-vector-empty path reachable.
attack_scenario: An attacker crafts a Sass/SCSS file whose `@extend` or selector rules cause `extender.cpp:permutate(extendedNotExpanded)` to be called with an inner vector that is empty; each such request leaks `(L+1) * sizeof(size_t)` bytes, enabling memory exhaustion in server-side libsass deployments that process user-supplied stylesheets.

proof_trace: `permutateAlt()` allocates `state` via `new size_t[L]` at line 79. The initialization loop (lines 83–86) contains an early return at line 84 (`if (in[i].size() == 0) return {}`) that exits without calling `delete[] state`. The only `delete[] state` at line 129 is reachable only via the normal `break` path; no RAII or smart pointer prevents the leak.
attack_scenario: An attacker supplies SCSS input that causes `permutateAlt()` to be invoked with a vector containing an empty inner vector (e.g., an empty parent-reference selector list), triggering the early return at line 84 and leaking the heap-allocated `state` array; repeated such requests cause unbounded heap growth leading to memory exhaustion (DoS).
